title: STOPSIGNAL ignored
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**I execute podman command as a non-root user.**
The STOPSIGNAL step in the Dockerfile is ignored when the container is being stopped.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Write a simple Dockerfile:
```
FROM centos:centos8
STOPSIGNAL 2
```

2. Build an image:
```
podman build -t signaltest .
```

3. Start a container with that image, wait a few seconds then hit Ctrl+C:
```
 podman run -ti -u root --name signal_test localhost/signaltest /bin/bash -c 'trap "echo SIGINT" SIGINT ; sleep 1000000'
^CSIGINT
```
So far so good.

4. Start a container with that image:
```
podman run -ti -u root --name signal_test localhost/signaltest /bin/bash -c 'trap "echo SIGINT" SIGINT ; sleep 1000000'
```
Wait a few seconds, open another window to the machine, double-check the stop signal:
```
podman inspect signal_test --format '{{json .Config.StopSignal}}'
2
```
Then stop the container:
```
 time podman stop signal_test
9f98d5b10e887c8dcbe52d0799793f5e4009400bcea83e8144df8c18b3d6f761

real    0m10.495s
user    0m0.140s
sys     0m0.275s
```

5. Go back to the first screen and I see no output. The container is stopped.

**Describe the results you received:**
What I see is that it took a little more than 10 seconds to stop the container. I understand that podman sent a SIGTERM first, after 10 second a SIGKILL.
The process in the container never received the required SIGINT (STOPSIGNAL 2)

**Describe the results you expected:**
Stopping the container with `podman stop` should have the same output as hitting Ctrl+C.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

This scenario is a reproduction of a more complex setup where the process in the container has to be stopped gracefully, i.e. by receiving a SIGINT.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.0.6
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.13.4
Built:        Tue Sep  8 21:37:13 2020
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

comments.body: Ok it looks like the StopSignal is making its way into the `podman inspect`  of the container.
Can you provide the output of `podman stop --log-level=debug` on the container?

Also, more environment details would be appreciated. Are you running as root or rootless, are you running the Podman remote client, etc...

Can you also check and make sure that SIGTERM is being sent, using a similar command but trapping SIGTERM instead?

Podman is definitely doing the right thing.

It is calling crun with this command.

/usr/bin/crun kill 6f1d5073e6130bbc85824592e4fad98e14ecfe8b398cc32dafc07bbada5f8dc4 2

./bin/podman run --rm -ti -u root --name signal_test localhost/signaltest /bin/bash -c 'trap "echo SIGQUIT" SIGQUIT ; sleep 1000000'

podman stop -l

I do not see any signal hitting the bash script.

It seems like the sleep is causing bash to ignore signals, Try Setting the StopSignal to 10, (SIGUSR1) Then use read.  Signal 2 (SIGQUIT) is not able to be trapped.

```
./bin/podman run --rm -ti -u root --name signal_test localhost/signaltest /bin/bash -c 'trap "echo SIGUSR1" SIGUSR1 ; read'
```
Second Terminal
```
./bin/podman stop -l
```
First terminal now shows:
```
./bin/podman run --rm -ti -u root --name signal_test localhost/signaltest /bin/bash -c 'trap "echo SIGUSR1" SIGUSR1; read'
SIGUSR1
```
